planets.name: Mercury
planets.description: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun and due to its proximity it is not easily seen except during twilight. For every two orbits of the Sun, Mercury completes three rotations about its axis and up until 1965 it was thought that the same side of Mercury constantly faced the Sun. Thirteen times a century Mercury can be observed from the Earth passing across the face of the Sun in an event called a transit, which occured on the 9th May 2016.
facts: Mercury is the smallest planet.
Mercury does not have any moons or rings.
Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun.
Your weight on Mercury would be 38% of your weight on Earth.
A solar day on the surface of Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.
Mercury is the second densest planet.
A year on Mercury takes 88 Earth days.
It remains one of the least explored planets, visited only by Mariner 10 (1974–75) and MESSENGER (2011–15), with ESA/JAXA's BepiColombo mission currently en route.

planets.name: Venus
planets.description: Venus is the second planet from the Sun and is the second largest terrestrial planet. Venus is sometimes referred to as the Earth's sister planet due to their similar size and mass. Venus is named after the Roman goddess of love and beauty.
facts: Venus is the hottest planet in our solar system.
Venus does not have any moons or rings.
Venus rotates in the opposite direction to most other planets.
Venus is the second brightest object in the night sky.
Venus is nearly as big as the Earth with a diameter of 12,104 km.
Venus is also known as the Morning Star and the Evening Star.
A day on the surface of Venus (solar day) would appear to take 117 Earth days.
A year on Venus takes 225 Earth days.

planets.name: Earth
planets.description: Earth is the third planet from the Sun and is the largest of the terrestrial planets. The Earth is the only planet in our solar system not to be named after a Greek or Roman deity. The Earth was formed approximately 4.54 billion years ago and is the only known planet to support life.
facts: The Earth is the densest planet in the Solar System.
Earth has a powerful magnetic field.
The Earth's rotation is gradually slowing.
The Earth was once believed to be the centre of the universe.
70% of the Earth's surface is covered in water.
Earth doesn't take 24 hours to rotate on its axis — it's actually 23 hours, 56 minutes and 4 seconds.
The Earth tilts at roughly 66 degrees.
Only 3% of Earth's water is fresh; the rest 97% is salted. Of that 3%, over 2% is frozen in ice sheets and glaciers.

planets.name: Mars
planets.description: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun and is the second smallest planet in the solar system. Named after the Roman god of war, Mars is also often described as the "Red Planet" due to its reddish appearance. Mars is a terrestrial planet with a thin atmosphere composed primarily of carbon dioxide.
facts: Mars and Earth have approximately the same landmass.
Mars is home to the tallest mountain in the solar system.
Mars has the largest dust storms in the solar system.
On Mars the Sun appears about half the size as it does on Earth.
One day Mars will have a ring.
Mars takes its name from the Roman god of war.
Sunsets on Mars are blue.
There are signs of liquid water on Mars.

planets.name: Jupiter
planets.description: Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system and is the fifth planet out from the Sun. It is two and a half times more massive than all the other planets in the solar system combined. It is made primarily of gases and is therefore known as a "gas giant".
facts: Jupiter is the fourth brightest object in the solar system after the Sun, Moon and Venus.
Jupiter has the shortest day of all the planets.
Jupiter orbits the Sun once every 11.8 Earth years.
The Great Red Spot is a huge storm on Jupiter.
Jupiter's moon Ganymede is the largest moon in the solar system.
Jupiter has a thin ring system.

planets.name: Saturn
planets.description: Best known for its fabulous ring system, Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and the second largest in our solar system. Like Jupiter, Saturn is a gas giant and is composed of similar gasses including hydrogen, helium and methane.
facts: Saturn is the most distant planet that can be seen with the naked eye.
Saturn has more moons than any other planet.
Saturn is the flattest planet.
Saturn has the most extensive rings in the solar system.
Saturn is made mostly of hydrogen.
Saturn has oval-shaped storms similar to Jupiter's.

planets.name: Uranus
planets.description: Uranus is the seventh planet from the Sun. It's not visible to the naked eye, and became the first planet discovered with the use of a telescope. Uranus is tipped over on its side with an axial tilt of 98 degrees. It is often described as "rolling around the Sun on its side."
facts: Uranus was officially discovered by Sir William Herschel in 1781.
Uranus is often referred to as an "ice giant" planet.
Uranus hits the coldest temperatures of any planet.
Uranus has two sets of very thin dark coloured rings.
Uranus' moons are named after characters created by William Shakespeare and Alexander Pope.

planets.name: Neptune
planets.description: Neptune is the eighth planet from the Sun, making it the most distant in the solar system. This gas giant may have formed much closer to the Sun in the early solar system history before migrating out to its current position.
facts: Neptune is the most distant planet from the Sun.
Neptune is the smallest gas giant.
A year on Neptune lasts 165 Earth years.
Neptune is named after the Roman god of the sea.
Neptune has 6 faint rings.
Neptune has a very active climate.

stars.name: Sun
stars.description: The Sun is the star at the centre of our solar system. It is an almost perfect sphere of super-hot gases whose gravity holds the solar system together. The energy produced by the Sun is essential for life on Earth and is a driving force behind the Earth's weather.
facts: The Sun is all the colours mixed together; this appears white to our eyes.
The Sun is composed of hydrogen (70%) and helium (28%).
The Sun contains 99.86% of the mass in the Solar System.
The Sun is an almost perfect sphere.
Light from the Sun takes eight minutes to reach Earth.
The Sun generates solar wind.
The temperature inside the Sun can reach 15 million degrees Celsius.
The Sun is a main-sequence G2V star (or Yellow Dwarf).
The Sun is 109 times wider than the Earth and 330,000 times as massive.
The Sun's surface area is 11,990 times that of the Earth's.
The distance between the Earth and the Sun is an Astronomical Unit (AU).
Sol is the Latin for Sun; this is where the word "solar" comes from.

moons.name: The Moon
moons.description: The Moon (or Luna) is the Earth’s only natural satellite and was formed 4.6 billion years ago around some 30–50 million years after the formation of the solar system. The Moon is in synchronous rotation with Earth meaning the same side is always facing the Earth. The first uncrewed mission to the Moon was in 1959 by the Soviet Lunar Program with the first crewed landing being Apollo 11 in 1969.
facts: The rise and fall of the tides on Earth is caused by the Moon.
The Moon is drifting away from the Earth.
A person would weigh much less on the Moon(about 1/6 th of your weigth on Earth
The Moon has no atmosphere.
The Moon has quakes.
The Moon is the fifth largest natural satellite in the Solar System.
The temperature inside the Sun can reach 15 million degrees Celsius.
The Sun is a main-sequence G2V star (or Yellow Dwarf).
The Sun is 109 times wider than the Earth and 330,000 times as massive.
The Sun's surface area is 11,990 times that of the Earth's.
The distance between the Earth and the Sun is an Astronomical Unit (AU).
Sol is the Latin for Sun; this is where the word "solar" comes from.
